title: How Frontend Talks to Backend
intro: A developer is trying to send data from their React frontend to their Express backend, but the request fails. The backend never receives the data. The bug is in how they set up the fetch request.

teach.title: Frontend-Backend Communication = Passing Notes
teach.body: The frontend and backend are like two people in different rooms passing notes. They need to agree on: **the language** (JSON), **the format** (where to put the message), and **the delivery method** (HTTP method like GET or POST). If any of these are wrong, the message doesn't get through.

teach.title: Frontend-Backend Communication = Ordering with a Walkie-Talkie
teach.body: Imagine a construction site where the crane operator (frontend) needs supplies from the warehouse (backend). They communicate over a **walkie-talkie** (fetch/HTTP). The operator must say the right code words: 'Requesting 50 bricks, section B' (the right method, URL, and body). If they say 'bricks please' without specifying a section, the warehouse won't know where to deliver. Precise communication is everything.

teach.title: How fetch() Works
teach.body:
The frontend uses `fetch()` to talk to the backend:

1. **URL** -- where to send the request (`/api/users`)
2. **Method** -- what kind of request (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE)
3. **Headers** -- metadata like `Content-Type: application/json`
4. **Body** -- the data you're sending (must be a JSON string)

If you forget to set the Content-Type header or forget to stringify the body, the backend won't understand your message.

teach.title: A Correct fetch() POST Request
teach.annotation: Three things are essential: the method must be POST, the Content-Type header tells the server to expect JSON, and the body must be a string (JSON.stringify converts an object to a JSON string).

teach.body: The connection between frontend and backend is the plumbing of every SaaS app, marketplace, and online store. Get this right when you vibecode, and you can wire up any feature -- payments, dashboards, notifications -- that turns your app into a business.

The frontend communicates with the backend using fetch() or similar HTTP clients. When sending data, always set the Content-Type header to 'application/json' and convert your data to a string with JSON.stringify().

exercise.prompt: The backend receives an empty body with no data. The developer set the Content-Type header correctly and used the POST method, but the data isn't arriving. The problem is on the line that sets the request body. Find the bug.

fixes.text: body: JSON.stringify({ name: name, email: email })

exercise.explanation: The fetch() API requires the body to be a string, not a JavaScript object. When you pass a plain object, it gets converted to '[object Object]' -- which is useless. You must use JSON.stringify() to convert the object into a proper JSON string like '{"name":"Alice","email":"alice@example.com"}'. This is one of the most common mistakes when first working with fetch().
